calendar.matters:
DEFENDANT OILVER MAULE'S MOTION TO QUASH (ADDED FOR THE POSTING OF THE COMPLETE TENTATIVE RULING)
Off Calendar Apr-10-2026 Text Ruling

MOTION TO QUASH SERVICE OF SUMMONS OR STAY OR DISMISS
Off Calendar Apr-10-2026 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: DEFENDANT OLIVER MAULE'S MOTION TO QUASH SERVICE OF SUMMONS

No appearances. The court adopts its tentative ruling as follows: Defendant Oliver Maule's motion to quash is denied.

When a defendant moves to quash service of process, the plaintiff bears the burden of demonstrating facts justifying the exercise of jurisdiction including proper service. (Dill v. Berquist Construction Co. (1994) 24 Cal.App.4th 1426, 1439-1440 ["[T]he burden is on the plaintiff to prove the existence of jurisdiction by proving, inter alia, the facts requisite to an effective service"].) Since 1969, statutes governing service are "liberally construed to effectuate service and uphold jurisdiction if actual notice has been received by the defendant." (Pasadena Medi-Center Associates v. Superior Court (1973) 9 Cal.3d 773, 778 [citation simplified].) "The Supreme Court's admonition to construe the process statutes liberally extends to substituted service as well as to personal service." (Bein v. Brechtel-Jochim Group, Inc. (1992) 6 Cal.App.4th 1387, 1392.)

Here, the court concludes that plaintiff effectuated personal service. Mr. Maule does not deny that he was on the premises on January 24, 2026, at the time of the attempted service. He also declares that no co-resident resides at the premises. Process server Smith declares that he triggered the ringcam, saw an individual inside respond through a window, and the individual attempted to ignore him and refused the documents. The process server called to the defendant by name and then left the papers at defendant's door. Based on this record, the only reasonable inference is that Mr. Maule was at the premises and attempted to avoid service.

Actual physical delivery of process is not necessary when a defendant is being recalcitrant. Cases recognize the sufficiency of attempted delivery in the presence of a defendant who refuses to accept the papers. (See In re Ball (1934) 2 Cal.App.2d 578, 579 [process server came within twelve fee of petitioner, said "I have here another one of those things for you," tossed paper toward the petitioner, and added "Now you are served"]; Trujillo v. Trujillo (1945) 71 Cal.App.2d 257, 260 [process server loudly explained nature of document through locked door of defendant's car and placed it under windshield wiper].) This case presents similar circumstances to these pre-1969 cases. Since service statutes are now liberally construed, the court finds proper personal service and denies the motion. Defendant shall respond to the complaint in compliance with CCP 418.10(b). Prevailing party to submit a proposed form of order. Judge: Christine Van Aken, Clerk: E. Larnauti, Not Reported (301/CVA)
